SuMoTu WeThFrSa
12:00 PM
meta

Markdown Kitchen Sink

A stress test for the theme. Everything markdown can do, in one place. Not meant to be read linearly — meant to be poked at.

Headings

Heading One

Heading Two

Heading Three

Heading Four

Heading Five
Heading Six

Prose & Inline Formatting

A plain paragraph set in Lora. The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog. Pack my box with five dozen liquor jugs. How vexingly quick daft zebras jump. Enough text to see line rhythm and spacing in action.

Bold text for emphasis. Italic text for a softer lean. Bold and italic together. Strikethrough for second thoughts. You can also write H2O with subscript and x^2^ with superscript, though those depend on plugins.

Mix them inline: a sentence with bold, then italic, then inline code, then a link to somewhere, all in a row.

Blockquotes

A simple single-line blockquote. Good for a short pull quote.

A longer blockquote to see how the left-border treatment holds up across multiple lines of wrapped text. Does it breathe well? Does the italic Lora feel right here? These are the questions.

A second paragraph inside the same blockquote. The spacing between paragraphs within a quote matters too.

Outer quote.

Nested inner quote. Indentation within indentation — a rare but valid case.

A third level, for the brave.

Lists

Unordered:

  • Apples
  • Oranges
  • Bananas
    • Cavendish
    • Plantain
      • Sweet plantain
      • Green plantain
  • Mangoes

Ordered:

  1. Wake up
  2. Make coffee
  3. Read something good
    1. Underline a line
    2. Dog-ear the page
  4. Go outside

Mixed nesting:

  1. First ordered item
    • An unordered child
    • Another unordered child
  2. Second ordered item
    1. An ordered grandchild

Task list:

  • Buy a Light Phone
  • Delete Twitter
  • Start a personal site
  • Write more often
  • Figure out the RSS feed
  • Add dark mode

Code

Inline: install dependencies with npm install, then run npm run dev.

A JavaScript block:

function formatDateShort(str) {
  const [y, m, d] = str.split('-').map(Number);
  const date = new Date(y, m - 1, d);
  const month = date.toLocaleDateString('en-US', { month: 'short' });
  return `${month} ${d}, '${String(y).slice(2)}`;
}

A CSS block:

:root {
  --bg:    #f5f1ea;
  --accent: #6a9e8a;
}

body {
  background-color: var(--bg);
  font-family: 'Lora', serif;
}

A plain text block (no language):

This is terminal output or a log line.
No syntax highlighting. Just monospace.
2026-04-26 12:00:01 INFO  Server started on :4321

Tables

A basic table:

BookAuthorYear
The Burnout SocietyByung-Chul Han2010
The Slow WebJack Cheng2012
A Pattern LanguageChristopher Alexander1977

Aligned columns:

Left-alignedCenteredRight-aligned
applebananacherry
onetwothree
shortmedium lengtha longer cell

Horizontal Rules

Three dashes:

Three asterisks:

Three underscores:

Links & Images

A basic link. A link with a title attribute. A bare URL: https://timhaley.me.

An image (using a local asset):

A Combined Example

Here is a blockquote that contains bold, italic, and code all at once:

The goal of refactoring is not to add features — it is to make the code easier to read and change.

And a list of quotes:

  1. “The cure for boredom is curiosity. There is no cure for curiosity.” — Dorothy Parker

  2. “You do not rise to the level of your goals. You fall to the level of your systems.” — James Clear

That’s the lot.

2:15 PM
thoughts

Walked outside without headphones today. Forgot how much ambient sound there is — birds, wind, someone’s lawnmower two streets over. Felt like a small act of rebellion.

8:44 PM
links

The Slow Web

This piece from 2012 holds up. The distinction between timely and real-time still feels sharp. Most of what I was checking compulsively was real-time — and completely replaceable with timely.

10:01 AM
thoughts

Testing out the new feed. Going off social media and putting thoughts here instead. We’ll see how this goes.

3:47 PM
books

The Burnout Society — Byung-Chul Han

Short but dense. Han argues we’ve moved from a disciplinary society (you must do X) to an achievement society (you can do anything) — and the latter is more exhausting because the oppressor is yourself. The constant self-optimization loop never ends.

Relevant to why I got a Light Phone.

6:23 PM
thoughts

Day three with the Light Phone. The withdrawal is real but not what I expected — it’s not anxiety about missing things, it’s not knowing what to do with my hands.

11:05 AM
quotes

The cure for boredom is curiosity. There is no cure for curiosity.

— Dorothy Parker

7:32 PM
quotes

You do not rise to the level of your goals. You fall to the level of your systems.

— James Clear

9:14 AM
thoughts

Starting to think about what I actually want this site to be. Not a portfolio — I have no interest in performing productivity. More like a garden. Things growing, some of them weird.
